American Writers & Artists Inc.
* * * * *
May, 2006
What Copywriters and Graphic Designers Need to Know About
Working Together to Create Winning Packages
Speakers Include:
Denise Ford, Hostess
Lori Haller, Graphic Designer
Donna Doyle, Copywriter
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 51 --

2
DENISE: 	What Copywriters and Graphic Designers
Need to Know About Working Together to Create Winning
Packages. 	My name is Denise Ford and it is my pleasure
to welcome you to this call with our very special
guests. 	Joining us today is Lori Haller. 	Hello Lori.
LORI: 	Hello Denise.
DENISE: 	How are you doing?
LORI: 	I’m great. 	How about you guys?
DENISE: 	You’re fine? 	Are you fighting the
allergies?
LORI: 	Yes, I have a couple of allergies here
but nothing that’s going to stop me today.
DENISE: 	Excellent. 	And our other very special
guest is Donna Doyle. 	Hello Donna.
DONNA: 	Hello Denise. 	How are you today?
DENISE: 	I’m well. Thank you very much for
taking time from your schedule to join us today.
DONNA: 	My pleasure.
DENISE: 	Ladies, I’m going to ask you to tell
our listeners a little bit about yourselves. 	Lori,
let’s start with you. 	Some of our folks may recognize
you from various bootcamps because besides being a
master graphic designer that you are, you’ve also done
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 51 --

3
presentations for us, but could you just tell us a few
things about your background as a graphic designer.
LORI: 	Great. 	I own Shadow Oak Studio and I
create winning campaigns, brochures, magalogs, logos
and branding, just a few of the things I do for clients
all over the nation and work with a variety of very
talented copywriters. 	And I also do a lot of speaking
with AWAI and conduct a bootcamp every fall, so I have
a lot of fun doing all of this stuff.
DENISE: 	And in addition I think you have a
handful of little munchkins, don’t you?
LORI: 	Yes, three small boys running around
the studio and they keep things exciting.
DENISE: 	And I think this call is going to be
exciting ---
LORI: 	Oh yes,
DENISE: 	--- so thank you for joining us.
Kristin told us about our next special guest and this
is a person that Lori has worked with often. 	It is the
perfect match for this call. 	The goal of the call is
to have this conversation about what copywriters and
graphic designers need to know about working together
and so one of Lori’s major masterful partners is Donna
Doyle. 	Hello Donna again.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 51 --

4
DONNA: 	Hi.
DENISE: 	Can you tell us a little bit about
your background as a copywriter?
DONNA: 	Sure. 	I’ve been writing direct
response copy – gosh, I hate to say it, but it’s well
over 15 years by now. 	I really started in direct
response working for Prentice Hall in their business
and health and self-improvement division and they sold
a lot of their books through the mail and that’s where
I came in and really cut my teeth on becoming a
copywriter and there one of my first projects wound up
becoming a control for 10 years, which was really an
amazing feat and still is to this day. I think they
just stopped mailing it – oh gosh, maybe three or four
years ago. 	After that I worked for Medical Economics
for the Physicians’ Desk Reference. 	Also doing their
direct mail and writing some direct response copy and
learning a little bit of design there as well, although
I’m not a designer by any means but at least it really
exposed me to the different aspects of print production
and why it’s important to work well with a graphic
designer and all of that other good stuff that really
formed the foundation for when I went freelance. 	I
became a freelance direct response copywriter in 2000,
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 51 --

5
so I’ve been doing it for six years. 	I have clients
throughout the country and I’ve done some controls with
Lori for companies like NorthStar Nutritionals and
BioNutrigenics and those are mailing and doing very,
very well. 	Also I’m a copy critiquer for AWAI and I am
also a member of their copywriting-mentoring program.
DENISE: 	I know, it’s wonderful. 	Where do you
live these days, Donna?
DONNA: 	Where do I live?
DENISE: 	Yes.
DONNA: 	I live in New Jersey, right by the New
Jersey shore.
DENISE: 	That must be beautiful in summer and a
bit cold in the winter.
DONNA: 	Yes, yes, but actually we’re kind of
more mild where I live as opposed to New York City
because we are by the water, so we don’t get quite as
much snow, but that’s good.
DENISE: 	That is good. 	Well I hope you have a
nice cup of warm tea while we have this conversation
together.
DONNA: 	I sure do. 	I have it right here.
How’d you guess?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 51 --

6
DENISE: 	Well ladies, you both mentioned the
word “controls” and for designers and writers the idea
of creating something that has legs, that has long
lasting potential is a great desire and you both have
done it and you’ve done it together, so if we could
spend a few minutes talking about the dynamics of
effective partnerships. 	Let’s take some situations
where you’ve worked together on a particular project
and talk a little bit about the project and perhaps
about how you went back and forth together. 	What kinds
of interactions that you used to create these winning
packages. 	Does that seem reasonable?
LORI: 	That sounds great. 	You know, Donna, we
could start talking about our first project together
when we first met each other.
DONNA: 	Absolutely.
LORI: 	That was Weight Guard Plus, a few years
ago with Agora and I’d gotten a copy and I’d read it a
couple of times, getting ready to do my layouts and
Donna, I don’t know if you remember, but I asked the
Director if I could please contact this copywriter
because the copy was so excellent and I just couldn’t
wait to talk to this person because I had so many ideas
and I wanted to run them past him or her and so they
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 51 --

7
gave me Donna’s name and phone number and I was able to
give her a call.
DONNA: 	And that’s really when it started for
us. 	I had already been freelancing for quite a while
as well as Lori and it was really this client that kind
of put us together.
LORI: 	Right.
DONNA: 	And we just hit it off. 	We were able
to brainstorm ideas really, really well and I was
always a great believer that a copywriter should always
work with some kind of designer for several reasons. 	I
mean, one is that when I write a magalog I have things
in my head as far as layout, the way I think things
should look and if I work with the designer I can
translate that into the graphic design so that things
flow much more smoothly, a sidebar is not placed
opposite copy where it wouldn’t make any sense.
Whereas, if you just let go of the copy and just give
it to the client and the client takes care of the
design and you’re not involved, mistakes happen and
mailings fail and I’ve seen it in my own experience and
I really feel that being able to work with Lori on
several projects made them so much stronger, so much
better and we have controls to prove it so I think it
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 51 --

8
really makes such a big difference with the
brainstorming and the ideas and everything else.
LORI: 	Well, that Weight Guard Plus one at
first, I don’t know if you remember, but we started it
in a one column format, the client was kind of kicking
around that look and then we decided to show them our
two column format and we ended up even just going with
two color, just blue and black and to this day, unless
I don’t have the right data, it’s still beating the
control 2 to 1 ---
DONNA: 	Absolutely.
LORI: 	--- which we’re really excited about
and on just the back and forth I think on that
beginning one for us together, I started freelancing
about the same time you did, six or seven years ago,
and I think just the back and forth on that one was our
first time that we could really show them how you could
work together and make these controls, as well as some
of the other ones. 	We have that Okinawa package that
we did together and the same thing, with the photo
choice, the position of copy, I think the way we
interact together as a team rather than one person just
doing one thing and going off and the other person
going in another direction, that’s really what gets us
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 51 --

9
those big controls and gets us asked back again by the
clients.
DONNA: 	Oh yes, and there are things that we
will point out to the client that the client might have
totally forgotten about. 	I mean, one is a very recent
promotion that Lori and I were just discussing before
the call that we did for Dragon Door Publishing and
it’s at the printers now, but when we were putting the
layout and the copy together, you know, Lori’s going
through it and she’s laying out the offer and she goes,
“What about a speed reply bonus?”
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	And I’m like, that’s right, I totally
forgot about that and we discussed it amongst ourselves
and then discussed it with the client and in the end we
decided not to go with the speed reply bonus as this
was a different type of thing that we were offering,
but just being able to have that kind of interaction
and coming up with different ideas that we can show the
client, “Hey, here’s something you might not have
thought of”.
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	“Here are some ideas for what this
speed reply bonus might be” and it’s better for us to
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 51 --

10
be proactive and show that we work as a strong team and
alert the client to different things that they might
not have even thought of.
DENISE: 	Right.
LORI: 	Especially sometimes when copy can go
on for months and months and Donna and the client and
their whole team are reviewing round after round of
versions and ideas. 	I get the final copy and it’s just
astounding sometimes that I’ll notice something that
just won’t be brought up because people have seen it
100 times and it’s really fun and refreshing to be able
to go, “Do you want this title as your premium or your
free report or did you want to switch back to the other
one?” and just that whole teamwork and working together
to get those controls in the end. 	That’s really,
really a strong point of working together as a team.
DENISE: 	Well now with Dragon Door it sounds
like that was an idea - suggestion that came from you
about a premium. 	Is that right Lori?
LORI: 	Yes. 	Actually I was just wondering –
you know, I have a variety of quality-controlled checks
that Donna and I do on all the things we do together
and I was just wondering where the quick reply bonus
was. 	Normally we’d do that and Donna and I just sat
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 51 --

11
down and talked about it and we presented it to the
client and then in the end like she mentioned, he told
us that we were going to try it as a test, this way
without one and then test that later after we got the
first set of results. 	So, being able to, I guess
Denise, talk freely with one another and Donna and I
have such a great rapport. 	She sets the stage so I
feel comfortable saying, “You know I tried that red for
that headline and it just popped too much. 	See what
you think about this new look. 	I kind of toned it down
to a burgundy” and she’ll get back to me. 	Without that
kind of bonding or relationship I just can’t see how
you could possibly get these huge controls or access to
new clients. 	They love it when they have a team they
can count on.
DONNA: 	Absolutely and there have been so many
times where Lori has recommended me to clients and I
have recommended Lori to clients. 	I mean, Dragon Door
is another very good example of that. 	I had been
speaking to John Du Cane, the President, off and on for
about two years about different projects and when he
hired me for this one he said, “Now, I need a really
good heavy experienced direct response graphic
designer. 	Do you know of someone?” And I’m like, “Of
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 51 --

12
course I do, Lori Haller and we work so well together”
and I gave John a bit of the history and Lori was hired
as well. That also happened with a recent project we
did for Healthy Directions which used to be Phillips.
I was hired to write new copy for one of their products
and I gave Lori a plug to become the designer and she
was.
LORI: 	And you know, at the same time they
were interviewing me and looking at my samples and
asked who were the copywriters that I hold controls
with and really have good teamwork going and I gave
Donna’s name out and that’s really how it happened.
That’s how you get your foot in the door ---
DONNA: 	That’s right.
LORI: 	--- and meet these people because I had
really been trying to get a piece to do with them for
about 10 years as that was my goal or my dream. 	It
just hadn’t happened yet. 	It wasn’t the right time and
this was really very exciting to me to be called by
them and have a recommendation from Donna. 	We had a
great time doing the piece.
DONNA: 	Yes we did.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 51 --

13
DENISE: 	It sounds like you also work on a
variety of projects together, not one particular niche
and that must be interesting.
DONNA: 	Oh yes it is, like for example, the
Okinawa package or what we call the “Okinawa Package”.
It’s actually an anti-aging supplement that’s very high
in anti-oxidants so with that, you had the whole
ancient secret of Asia, Okinawa theme running through
it which, from a design standpoint, Lori was able to
have fun with and I was able to have fun with from a
copy standpoint. 	And really, having the opportunity to
work on so many different types of products is great
because you never get stale.
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	I mean there’s a lot of joint health
supplements out there; there’s a lot of the same type
of products out there and by constantly moving onto
different things you always can have a fresh approach
and you also learn from things done in the past and
that was another thing that helps so much when you work
with a colleague because there might be something that
I tried and Lori can say to me, “Well, you know, I know
in a mailing that I did for another client a year ago,
they tried that very same thing and it really didn’t
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 51 --

14
work” or “You know what, Donna, we really need a couple
of graphs. 	Can you get together some information so we
can have a really good graph on page 16?”
LORI: 	Yeah, that’s what’s great, is that
Donna just comes right back and says, “You know, either
a graph would be great there and here’s the data” or
“You know, the graphs haven’t worked good in the past”
or “We’ve tried that before” and just that back and
forth and using the history or the data and bring it to
each client as we progress is just irreplaceable,
priceless.
DENISE: 	And you both have a sense of the whole
package. 	I mean, I thought that was interesting, you
both used that. 	It’s not that you’re totally focused
in your one area. 	You feel comfortable enough to make
suggestions in each other’s domain because you’re both
interested in creating the control.
DONNA: 	Yes, and also when I was working way
back when on staff, especially at Med. Ec. in Prentice
Hall, I was in a situation where I actually hired
freelancers and I worked very closely with other
designers so I already knew what to do and what not to
do when it came to working with freelancers and other
colleagues and that definitely translates into one of
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 51 --

15
the reasons why Lori and I work together so well now
because I understood how important having that
design/copy collaboration is.
DENISE: 	When you’re a freelancer you’re used
to working alone and many people understand the theory
that you can increase your income and your productivity
by working with a partner but can you both talk to us
about ways to do that? 	How can you work with a partner
and what kinds of things working together produce a
better scenario for you than working alone, like
communication suggestions, communication tools and how
presenting yourself as a team maybe commands higher
fees or something?
DONNA: 	Well, I definitely think it commands
higher fees because the client understands that they
are getting two extremely experienced people that are
giving their direct mail magalog the best shot it could
have. 	Unfortunately, there are a lot of client out
there that will maybe pay a fee on copy but they’ll
chintz on the design and vice versa and it’s usually –
from what I can see – it’s usually a mistake. 	In my
experience, there was a client that I had that I gave
the copy to. 	It was for an immunity product. 	I had no
input on the design whatsoever. 	I didn’t see it laid
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 51 --

16
out until I actually got the printed piece and I knew
the minute I looked at this that it was going to fail.
I knew it, just because of the way the design was.
DENISE: 	Oh gosh.
DONNA: 	And sure enough, it failed miserably.
And now I make it with this client that I say, “Look, I
need to have input on the design. There are certain
ways that I wrote this and I need to be able to
translate that to your designer”. 	And also too, when
you work freelance, as Lori I’m sure could attest to,
you work in a vacuum and having that communication and
collaboration with somebody else is so important and it
really helps us too because we’re able to really bounce
ideas across and I try to communicate that into things
I do with clients as well.
LORI: 	Right and what I think we also offer
them, another reason why we can really command higher
fees is it’s one-stop shopping really with us together.
If they need a new report cover because at the last
minute they decide, “You know what, we just got the
test results in, we’re on this deadline, that report on
the other test did not work. 	Donna, can you come up
with a quick new report cover? 	Lori, can you design
it, put it in there?” 	We can take care of everything.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 51 --

17
There’s no worry. 	They don’t have to worry about
missing their deadline.
DONNA: 	Right.
LORI: 	We’re right on top of it together ---
DONNA: 	And it’s less for the client to worry
about.
LORI: 	Yes. 	That, Donna, is probably one of
the biggest things. 	That they can count on us. 	I know
a lot of people will just give us – hook us both up and
just say, “This is when we want to get it out”. 	We
send them the proofs and we give them recommendations
and they can rest assured that we’ll meet their
deadline and give them the best package that we could
possibly come up with in the end.
DONNA: 	And there is a lot of times too that
with the communication between Lori and I, there is so
much that we discuss amongst ourselves that the client
doesn’t even know about but we feel that by doing that
we can really go back and say, “Okay, here’s the
rationale on why things were designed to the way they
were and why we put the sidebar here” and nine times
out of ten they’ll listen and say, “Okay, that makes a
lot of sense”.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 51 --

18
LORI: 	Right, because we have went back to our
history or notes and we’re bringing in the knowledge
together to help them make these decisions and some
people don’t even know – you know at certain places
that they’re busy or rushed or they have a lot of other
projects, they don’t even have the time to go over all
those checkpoints that we kind of do for them, so it
really works out well.
DENISE: 	And I guess you’re saying that if you
develop your skills as a writer or a designer there are
situations where a client will turn to you and say, “Do
you know a designer? 	Do you know a copywriter?”
DONNA: 	Absolutely.
LORI: 	Exactly.
DONNA: 	Absolutely. 	And that’s how your income
increases as well.
LORI: 	Right, and your clients table. 	I mean,
I have just increased my client list tremendously,
hooking up with, meeting and working with Donna and
other copywriters in this exact way. 	Meeting them,
meeting them either at bootcamp or other places, at
forums and then working together on a small project
perhaps and then going onto another project for a
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 51 --

19
person, then somebody else hears about the name and it
just works out really good for us.
DONNA: 	Yeah, I have found interestingly enough
that with some people that may have approached me
regarding a future project and say, “You know, I hear
your name all over the place”. And I’m like “Really?”
It’s nice knowing that but I think that’s where the
personal recommendations get you and I think that’s
where you know, Lori recommending me and me
recommending Lori and we have some really good controls
that are still mailing that we can say, “Yes, we worked
on these together. 	They became controls. 	We can do
this for you too”.
LORI: 	And if they hear also – I kind of keep
this in my head the whole time I’m working because I
have very high standards for myself and my company as
does Donna, but just that whole professionalism and our
communication level and how we handle deadlines and the
pressure that can come with that. 	We’re very easy to
get along with, we’re prompt, we’re always at our
offices and quickly email back. 	That’s another great
point along with all the other ones about Donna is that
she’s always there. 	If I have a quick question, you
know, “Are we going to test this second headline? 	What
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 51 --

20
do you think about this?” 	It’s not days and days that
passes which would be terrible for our deadlines.
She’s right back with me or she emails me or calls me
and we just keep moving forward to that final goal.
DENISE: 	So that when you’re working with a
collaborator you have to be able to collaborate.
DONNA: 	Absolutely.
DENISE: 	You have to be set up professionally
and you have to be in touch.
DONNA: 	Absolutely. 	You have to have those
lines of communications open. 	It’s all about mutual
trust and dependability. 	You know, you could be
working with the greatest writer or designer in the
world, but if they don’t get back to you, if they don’t
give you what you need when you need it, what good is
the relationship?
LORI: 	Or if they’re not easy to work with.
You know, that’s hard or it makes more pressure on some
of these demanding deadlines and I just think having us
as a resource I think that’s why people call us again
and again. 	We’re not just a copywriter or designer.
It’s kind of a whole one-stop shop and they can depend
on that.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 51 --

21
DENISE: 	So folks who are working on their own
and haven’t yet found their partners or collaborators,
it is important to begin to do that, right? 	To start
to develop a network?
LORI: 	Yes.
DONNA: 	Yes, absolutely. 	I’ve met the graphic
designers that I’ve worked with mostly through other
clients. 	Some of them – one or two of them were people
that I knew that I worked well with when I was on staff
but mostly I’ve met them through clients and I’ve met
them through clients only because I actually said,
“Hey, when this gets ready to design, my fee includes
working with your graphic designer, because I really
feel it’s so important for the writer to give input”
and they’ll say, “Okay, great, we’ll give you that
person’s name and when the time comes you can work
together” and it’s also a really good test to see if
you would make a good team with this other person.
LORI: 	Right, or maybe even trying something
else small and then working on a bigger project. 	You
know, Donna, you mentioned that you knew – you do, you
really bring to the table a lot of knowledge about art
and graphic design. 	Copywriters don’t in general,
Denise, have to know that but I kind of pride myself on
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 51 --

22
continuously trying to study copywriting or learning
about it or reading certain books and emails and going
to the bootcamps and sending myself to these things
because one, I need people that are copywriters that we
might be able to work together but then again I learn
different tactics about a headline or the strength of
the clothes or something. No, I’m not going to write
the copy but I will understand the wording or the
information that Donna’s trying to explain to me when
we’re doing a project together and I think that’s
helpful.
DONNA: 	And it’s extremely helpful from a copy
standpoint as well.
DENISE: 	Now ladies, let’s talk for a moment,
because you mentioned this briefly about those
challenging situations. 	Those times when you’re
working for a client and you’re trying to deliver what
their audience may need but you run into problems,
either in communication with the clients or in
resources or things like that. How do you handle
problems?
DONNA: 	Well, that’s one of those areas where
Lori and I will talk and talk and talk without the
client ever worrying about it. 	We will often try to
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 51 --

23
offset any problems that we can see in the past, but
like in any project things come up that you don’t
foresee, you know, and sometimes the deadlines are
changed or moved up. 	That’s when we really try to work
together as a team and try and solve it together.
LORI: 	And be really flexible - as flexible as
we can, and just remember the goal in the end. The goal
is to please the client – one of the goals, and get a
winning package and get this to mail on time because
you know, it’s so costly if they miss their mailing.
They’d be there mailing this – sometimes people don’t
realize the whole big picture – they might be mailing
this test that Donna and I are doing with three or four
other packages and if we fall behind and don’t meet
that mail date or that printing date we could cost them
so much money. 	So, even though, for example, we might
be a little more expensive than other people or our
rates have gone up since we’re commanding higher fees
working together, we can save the client so much money
and time in the end and meet these deadlines and help
them as things change.
DENISE: 	How do you deal with a client when you
both make a suggestion and the client rejects that
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 51 --

24
idea? 	How do you go about negotiating or convincing
the client that it’s a good choice?
DONNA: 	Well, sometimes you can’t. 	Sometimes
they just have an idea in their head and you know, I
have my rule of three which is where you suggest
something, the same thing three times and if they don’t
go for it by the third try, that’s it, you just let it
go. 	So, sometimes you can’t change their mind, they
just have it in their head and we are paid to please
the client so we do that. 	There are other times,
however, that we are able to negotiate and that’s where
our history together can come in, because we can point
– Lori can point to things that she’s done in the past
and I can as well, where we can say to the client,
“Well, that’s a great idea but in a package that Lori
did for another client three years ago they tried
something similar and it bombed”.
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	Or I can say to a client, “Well, that
is a great idea but I know of a way to make it work
even better because I know that for a certain mailer
they tried this idea and we made it work and now I can
make it work even better for you”.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 24 of 51 --

25
LORI: 	And that’s great – about technology
today is Donna can just say, “You know, Lori, can you
just try this other option and show them both things
next to each other” and that’s helpful too.
DENISE: 	So they can see it for themselves.
DONNA: 	You know, one thing that designers that
I work with always love and I recommend this for every
single copywriter is to buy ADOBE Acrobat – not Acrobat
Reader, but the full program ADOBE Acrobat and in
there, what a designer can do and what Lori does is
send me the PDF of how the layout is going to look and
they have a tool in there that looks like a little
yellow sticky where you can actually place the sticky
where you want it and then type exactly what changes
you make or if you want copy revised and it’s an
invaluable tool and it saves so much time than faxing
things over and “Oh, I can’t read your fax” or
explaining it in an email or wasting an hour on the
phone. 	That, I have to say, was probably the best
software investment I ever made.
LORI: 	Yes, I have to agree with that and it
makes us a cut above the rest I think.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 51 --

26
DENISE: 	And actually I can see how it can be
resourceful also for the client if you send them the
samples, two envelope copies or something.
DONNA: 	Oh yeah, and we’ve done that where the
client can see Lori’s remarks and my remarks and he’ll
add some of their own and it’s helpful for everybody.
DENISE: 	What do you do when you get a client
who says, “This is crap”? 	Excuse my French. 	But what
happens when they really tell you it’s terrible? 	How
do you respond to that?
DONNA: 	I would say what I would do is say,
“Well, why is it terrible? 	What can we do to make it
better?”
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	And then we just try and improve it
from there. 	Happily that doesn’t happen very often,
thank goodness.
LORI: 	One thing I think we try to do is I
kind of set the stage too, Donna – I think both of us
do, especially in those first drafts of PDF files. You
know, for months they’ve been seeing black typewriter
type on white pieces of paper. 	These people obviously
aren’t real visual people or they’d probably do it
themselves so it’s our goal to show them, give them a
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 51 --

27
vision and you can’t nail it the first time every time
so one thing that we try to do together is show a
variety of looks and I always, like I said, set the
tone and say, “These are three looks, three different
ways” and we give them a really strong approach, maybe
a softer color palette and then a less handsome looking
font that might really be good for this particular
product and it allows them to have a starting point.
Before that we had nowhere to begin but with these
white pieces of paper and black letters so I always
just say – I’ve had a couple of times when a client
said, “You know, I had something totally different in
mind. 	I was thinking orange and purple and really
flowery” and obviously with the copy I hadn’t caught
onto that. 	“No big deal. If that’s the direction you
want to take, let me take a couple of hours and put
together a couple more looks for you. I’ll work with
the copywriter and we’ll get something right over to
you”. 	That’s the kind of back and forth Donna and I do
and that really – that’s the kind of teamwork that I
think they require and they demand.
DONNA: 	Yes and with the fees that they’re
paying they should expect to get, frankly, because it
is always still about pleasing the client. 	It’s about
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 51 --

28
customer service, for lack of a better term, but it is.
And they are paying us our fees because they expect the
best so we want to be able to let them feel comfortable
that they are getting the best and this is something
that is using tried and true technique of direct
response that have worked in the marketplace.
DENISE: 	And you both clearly understand the
difference between accepting criticism and not taking
it personally.
DONNA: 	Absolutely.
LORI: 	Oh right.
DONNA: 	You can’t take it personally. 	You
can’t.
LORI: 	These are professional comments and we
only take them in that respect and you know, “Let us
think about this. 	Let us get back to you. 	Maybe we
weren’t on the same page of that whole look. Let us try
something” and that has always seemed to work really,
really well. 	And in the end, you know, sometimes –
some idea - I’ve had this happen a couple of times,
this totally different idea that someone on the team
might come up with after seeing a couple of layouts is
the winner, killer idea and if you wouldn’t have given
everybody a chance or shown a variety of ideas or taken
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 28 of 51 --

29
the time, you would have never gotten to that nugget of
information, so it’s very, very important to have this
back and forth, even if it isn’t the best sometimes.
DENISE: 	That’s a very good suggestion and it
does sound like whether you’re working in a partnership
or not, that’s an important skill to develop in dealing
with a client and feedback.
DONNA: 	Yes, and sometimes, believe it or not,
sometimes the client really is right. You know? 	Once
you get past the “What do you mean this stinks? 	How
could you say that?” and then you just get some
emotional distance from it and look upon it just as
objectively as you can. 	You can say, “Gee, you know,
he’s got a point” and then that I find improves
everything in the piece because then you see other
places in the copy that could be made stronger or could
expound on this theme a bit more and then it just makes
the design flow that much better as well, so it’s
really a win-win for everybody I think.
LORI: 	Yeah, no-one in any business wants to
work with a prima donna person who only – you know, the
artist that only will have it their way or the
copywriter that’s unbendable or whatever, for the
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 29 of 51 --

30
general good so I think that shows them something else
about us too. 	Don’t you Donna?
DONNA: 	Absolutely.
LORI: 	You know, we’re willing to work
together and we will listen. 	We’re very good listeners
and we take direction well.
DENISE: 	That’s a key technique. 	We’ll go into
techniques in one minute here, but I have one more
question and this is actually a copywriter who was
ecstatic to get their first client, asked us about.
What happens when you put a package together, it’s
tested and it bombs? 	What do you do, each of you or
together? 	How do you regroup and move forward?
DONNA: 	Well, after crying ---
LORI: 	I was just going to say that, we cry a
lot.
DONNA: 	That actually happened to us not too
long ago, right Lori?
LORI: 	Yeah, we took a little spill. 	We were
pretty pumped up and it was pretty much 99% sure we
were going to win this one – it’s huge and see, I try
never to say something’s going to win really huge and
be so confident. 	I’m sure I jinxed us but ---
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 30 of 51 --

31
DONNA: 	Well, I think you know, I gave that a
lot of thought and I remember Lori and I were on the
phone for an hour after I heard the news and what I
really came to the conclusion was that she and I gave
it our best shot. We delivered the best package that we
could and there are a lot of reasons why things fail in
the mail that have absolutely nothing to do with the
copy or the design. 	One of the problems with this
particular product was that it was in a marketplace
that was very, very crowded.
LORI: 	Right.
DONNA: 	So they had to really, really compete
to get their message heard. 	Another problem was that
they were extremely FDA compliant. 	Very, very aware to
the point where my hands were tied creatively in a lot
of ways as to things I could and could not say, so that
tended to affect the response levels as well. So things
happen. Sometimes you never know the reason why a
package fails. 	Sometimes you can point to things that
can, but we tried to regroup and we thought of things
that maybe could have been done better. 	I think one
thing was the size, Lori?
LORI: 	Right, the format I think – hindsight
obviously is so wonderful, as Brian Kurtz has said many
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 51 --

32
times in the boardroom, but yeah, I think the format. I
would give anything to retest the format again and just
see. 	That could have a lot to do with it, but the
other thing – you just said something really important
Donna, is that we did our very best and they will come
back to us and they already have.
DONNA: 	Yeah.
LORI: 	If we would have said, “Well, we can’t
change this photo or this copy because gosh, you’re
screwing up all the neat fancy stuff we were doing. 	I
don’t care if you want to FDA comply or whatever”, well
they would never call us back, but being helpful and
resourceful and working together, you know, they’re
going to call us back. 	We’re not going to win every
single one but we want to, but they’ll remember us on a
good note.
DONNA: 	Yes, and that we were easy to work
with, that we were very flexible to their ideas and
most importantly that we met every single one of their
deadlines.
DENISE: 	Right, that’s good. So you do do a
little bit of post-mortem?
DONNA: 	Oh yeah.
LORI: 	Oh yeah.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 51 --

33
DENISE: 	Right, and you try to analyze the
situation and then take some analysis from that moving
forward, even if you don’t get to apply it to that
particular product?
DONNA: 	Correct.
LORI: 	Right.
DENISE: 	Very good.
DONNA: 	And we also learn too, for when we deal
with other clients. 	You know, now in the future if I
have a client that says to me, “Well, I’m thinking of
launching a joint product or this heart product and I’m
not sure which one”, I can go back and say, “Well, you
know, the joint supplement is a very, very crowded
marketplace. It has to be really different, you might
want to consider this other one” or if we have a client
that wants to try a strange format again, we can say,
“Well, hey, we have a history of trying different
formats and they don’t work” and so we do learn from
it.
LORI: 	I ---
DENISE: 	I’m sorry, go head Lori.
LORI: 	I was just going to tell you quickly
Denise, there’s one thing that I did that might be
helpful to the listeners is I follow up with a thank
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 51 --

34
you note, and I know Donna does stuff like that too,
and then if they call me back – you know, I thank them
for giving me the opportunity to work with them,
whether it was the first time or the 100th time but I
also, when I talk to them again, maybe the post-mortem,
they want to tell me that it either won or didn’t, I
always say to them and also the production staff – on
the art end it’s a little different than the copy end,
but I need to deal with their printer, their in-house
art team, their illustrator, their this, their that. I
always make a point to send something to each person
and thank them but also tell them, you know, “We’re
here to service you and help you and be a part of your
team in the future. 	If there’s any software, anything
that we can do differently we are dying to know. 	I’d
love to hear about it” and I’ve even had people tell me
that they’ve bought some new pre-flight art software
and they were wondering maybe if we were going to buy
it too and so we went and bought this stuff and it just
seems like it is a good follow up and it let’s them
know that you mean business.
DENISE: 	Yeah, that’s good and that’s part of
the techniques for keeping your skills sharp, that kind
of communication process, isn’t it?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 34 of 51 --

35
LORI: 	Right.
DENISE: 	Now, both of you mentioned other things
like you’re available to your clients by maintaining
business hours and that it’s critical to listen
carefully, not just respond and be open to taking
direction. 	Can you give us some other techniques for
keeping your collaboration skills sharp?
DONNA: 	Well, I think it’s very important to
meet your deadlines and if for some reason that you
can’t, let the client know in plenty of time.
DENISE: 	That’s great.
DONNA: 	You know, if you think there’s going to
be a problem – if they expect to have something you
know, May 1st and April 30th you decide it’s not going
to be done, don’t let them know April 30th. 	Try to
schedule things that you can kind of predict, “Mmm, I’m
running into a little trouble on this, let me see if I
can get an extra day or two” and try to let them know
several days in advance. 	Nine times out of ten,
that’ll be okay with the client because they want to
have the best product possible, you know but you still
want to try and make all of your deadlines and be
respectful to what they have to say and to also know
their product. 	You have to know their product, know
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 35 of 51 --

36
their audience but that’s a lot of basic copywriter
stuff as well.
LORI: 	That’s a great point though Donna,
because a lot of people don’t realize how much research
a graphic designer needs to do or should do on their
end. 	When somebody hooks Donna and I up for a project
and they tell me it’s on joint or heart or whatever it
is, even before I get the copy I will go ahead and go
on the Internet or go to the bookstore, the library and
do tons of research myself so I understand what this
copy could be talking about. 	I read two papers every
day. 	I want to make sure I’m on the cutting edge of
knowing what’s going on out there in our society; not
just in this stuff, but you know, arts, theatre,
fashion, all that stuff depicts the colors and tones
and themes that we will use in some of these magalogs.
DONNA: 	Yeah, when Lori and I – when we’re
ready to get to the design stage, before Lori starts
anything she and I will have a very long phone call,
“Who was the market? What makes this product exciting?
When you were writing the copy what kind of themes do
you have?” – she’ll have my copy in front of her and
then that’s when we’ll start brainstorming things back
and forth.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 36 of 51 --

37
LORI: 	Right. 	Sometimes we try to become that
person. 	You know, am I a man that has this hip joint
or heart problem? 	Am I in my 60s? 	Have I responded to
a couple of other things? 	Would I want this report and
this product? 	What do I do or what colors would prompt
me to continue reading other than just the cover? My
goal, or our goal together then is make them get to
that order form and sign up and give us that order.
Those kinds of things we talk about in the very
beginning and through the whole process.
DONNA: 	Another thing too is sometimes what a
designer looking at copy will think is important might
not be important at all. 	It might be something else
that should be expounded on and stressed a lot more.
I’ve run into that a couple of times and by being able
as a writer to work with the designer you can lead them
in the right direction as well.
LORI: 	Right, because when we get the copy on
the art end we don’t know that sidebar number seven is
really supposed to be the punchiest, biggest one. 	Now,
Donna’s very good because she tells you so much
information in the notes and it’s just so easy, but
that is really important. 	I don’t want to just design
to make this pretty piece for my portfolio. 	I’m
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 37 of 51 --

38
designing and creating for a reason. 	We want to gain
access to these people and have them sign up so I need
to think about the copy very seriously. 	Copy is the
king here and not the fancy design or flourishes that I
might add just for my own well-being.
DONNA: 	However, good design is extremely
important to making the copy work.
LORI: 	I’m so glad you said that Donna.
DONNA: 	Yeah, but it’s true. 	It’s true. 	I
mean, just looking at the story I told before about
that mailing that I knew the minute I saw the printed
piece it wasn’t going to work because the design was so
bad. 	Reading the letter it was so difficult to track,
there were no photos. 	Now, if they didn’t put photos
in because the copy was too long, that’s the kind of
thing as a writer I could have easily changed.
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	But instead they throw everything in
but the kitchen sink; they didn’t treat the sidebars
correctly and the package failed.
LORI: 	But that’s what’s so great about
getting feedback from each other along the way. You
know, suggestions about “Perhaps this might be too long
here Donna, that maybe I should shrink the size of this
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 38 of 51 --

39
photo” or “That’d be great Lori, that way we can get
that copy in” and just those kind of things I think are
really helpful.
DONNA: 	And the way Lori knows about copy and
what makes good copy I think it’s a big, big help to
her that I know something about design and production.
LORI: 	Yes.
DONNA: 	I think writers should always have a
working knowledge of Quark or InDesign. You know, those
software programs used to make layouts and using
Acrobat Professional and also knowing a little bit
about print production. 	I mean, you don’t have to be
an expert at it by any means, but just to have a
working knowledge of why things have to be laid out the
way they are. What’s the difference between CMYK and
RGB? 	You know, these are the type of things as a
writer you should know and it’s another skill that you
can offer your clients. 	I have a couple of clients
that want me to handle their printing as well.
DENISE: 	Oh, that’s great.
DONNA: 	You know, if I didn’t have that
knowledge, that’s business that I would be losing so it
really is kind of a nice change reaction.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

printing. 	People these days, with the shortage of
income sometimes when these companies, they need to
come to you and know, “Oh my gosh, I need this done,
whatever it is and I know Donna can take care of it or
Lori can take care of it. I know it’s in good hands. 	I
can trust that what I need to get done will get done”.

DONNA: 	Absolutely.

execute this product?
DONNA: 	That’s right. 	I mean, I have done that
on many, many occasions and nothing leaves my office
until I’m 100% satisfied with it so I know that in the
end the client will always get the best possible
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 40 of 51 --

41
product that they will get. 	You know, one good example
is web marketing. 	I’ve done a lot of email direct
response by now, but I don’t know a lot about search
engine optimization or the best ways to have web
marketing, but I have a great collaborator that does.
DENISE: 	Right.
DONNA: 	So I will give him a call and you know,
ask him to help me out with certain things, so it
really does increase your business tremendously if
you’re able to have several people collaborate with
you.
LORI: 	And just the partnerships, you know,
whether it’s in copy or design, illustration, and it
does really, really help, I believe. 	I’ve seen a lot
of people at our bootcamps come to the graphic design
section that had already been to the copy one the year
before and they were really interested in trying to
hone their skills and vice versa. I’ve met many people
in the copywriting bootcamps that are going to the next
year send themselves to the graphic design one and I
think that’s very important. 	That’s the kind of stuff
I do to this day to try to stay on the cutting edge and
then within that build these partnerships.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 41 of 51 --

42
DENISE: 	And I think one of the things that I
really appreciate about the way you work – and this is
a very important technique – from the beginning you
state your mutual goals. 	You say to each other, “How
are we going to produce the control for this client?”
I mean that’s a great way to start a conversation
especially if you are working with someone for the
first time because then you’re both on the same team.
LORI: 	Right.
DONNA: 	Yes.
LORI: 	I’ve even known people that have just
met each other, either through a forum or some other
means and just decided, you know, this copywriter got
this first job doing a small brochure locally, they met
a designer, they emailed them, they said, “Here’s the
copy, I’ve been hired. 	They said I could pick my own
designer. 	Would you like to work with me?” And it can
start just like that Denise and then you keep working
together and showing people your work and your name
gets around and you can start doing these magalogs and
these bigger direct mail packages.
DENISE: 	Is it also useful – do you find that
during your process, when you’re working together, you
separate – I don’t know if you separate, but there are
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 42 of 51 --

43
times when you are brainstorming, sort of just coming
up with ideas and then there are times when you’re
taking specific pieces of the material and critiquing
it and taking it apart. 	Is it useful to have a free-
flow, like you said at the beginning Lori, you have a
conversation just back and forth and then you get into
actual nitty-gritty.
LORI: 	Yeah, you know, I think that’s how it
works really well for us. 	You figure, I haven’t been
in on the ten meetings that maybe the client or the
other associates or team members or Donna has been. I
need to quickly bring myself up to speed so a variety
of emails or sometimes people send me a packet and then
we have that nice conversation and I’ll throw out some
ideas. 	I’ll even do some – I’m doing this right now –
some quick sketches of how a magalog might look. 	I
haven’t even went to the computer yet. 	I’m showing
some ideas for illustrations that we might want to kick
around and you know, talking back and forth and that
kind of stuff is so important. 	I never just go right
to the computer and design the whole thing in a vacuum.
There’s no way I could even think of getting a winning
piece doing it that way.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 43 of 51 --

44
DONNA: 	Yeah, and I don’t think it would work
well from a copy standpoint either. 	Like, there was
one thing that we did in our Okinawa mailing – one
thing I’m fond of doing is having like a little
checklist, you know, that the client gets to compare
the products and how much better they feel after taking
it, and I was – you know, Lori saw the questionnaire
and she’s like, “Oh well, when I lay this out I’m going
to have like a dotted line around it that people can
cut out with scissors” and that’s exactly what I
wanted. 	That’s exactly what I had in mind and not
every designer, especially if you don’t speak with them
or have any input to the design, will even think of
that. 	They will just put a line or just box it. 	But
when you have just a little difference of putting that
dotted line makes it so much more interactive.
LORI: 	Right, and we try to do a lot of stuff
that way, whether it’s the premium, the look of the
premium or how we show the product or maybe a burst on
it. 	We try to make our things stand out.
DONNA: 	Yeah, typically Lori will show like a
few covers at first and then she might show one or two
spreads and then she’ll lay out the entire thing, right
Lori?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 44 of 51 --

45
LORI: 	Yeah, after you guys have had a chance
to look at it they might say, “You know what” – and
sometimes this happens with us – sometimes it’s
everybody’s intention to start the letter, “Dear
Friend” or “Dear Hip Replacement Person” or whatever it
is on the front and then I see the copy and I start
fooling around with it and of course I always show what
is expected or wanted but then if there’s an idea that
comes to mind I’ll just try to go for it. 	That’s kind
of what happened with the Weight Guard Plus, is that we
really, really had our hearts set on one column and we
just kept fooling around with it until it felt the way
that the way the copy read properly and it just seemed
like it panned out in the end and you know, obviously
by the numbers, it did.
DENISE: 	Well, I can understand how the two of
you have had such a successful working relationship and
I really thank you for your generous time. 	Before we
wrap this call up, is there something you would suggest
to our listeners about how they can go about forming
these kinds of working relationships?
DONNA: 	Well, I think as Lori mentioned, going
to things like bootcamp. 	It’s certainly a way to meet
other people in your business, whether you’re a writer
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 45 of 51 --

46
or a designer and you get to just be able to see from
someone’s personality if you’re going to have a rapport
or not. I mean, that’s a great way of doing it. 	You
know, also through your clients. 	If they happen to put
you up with a designer of their own. That’s another
good way to establish collaborations. 	People that you
might have worked with in previous jobs is another
thing. 	One of my best copy cheats is my former boss at
Prentice Hall.
DENISE: 	Oh, that’s great.
DONNA: 	And he’s fantastic and I would not be
as successful as I am without his input. 	All of that
is so invaluable and in time, it’s just networking.
It’s making contacts, it’s calling up people like Lori
and saying, “Hey, you know, I’m dying to know a
Webmaster. I have a client who needs a website design.
Can you help me? 	Can you recommend people?” And you
kind of branch out like that. Kind of like the branches
on a tree. 	It works very much the same way.
LORI: 	And actually, you had just asked me
that about three or four weeks ago ---
DONNA: 	That’s correct.
LORI: 	--- and low and behold one of our
artists from our recent AWAI bootcamp – two of them
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 46 of 51 --

47
actually had resources or worked in that department
that you were looking for and I wasn’t able to do it
myself and so I gave their name out and so they hooked
up with Donna and before that they didn’t possibly know
her yet, so I just think it’s an endless source of
contacts and people. 	I know you’re going to think this
is funny Denise, but even though I’m a speaker at
bootcamp, I always come and listen and talk with people
and meet them and every year I come home with four or
five contacts and do work with people during that year
which is very exciting meeting these people from all
over the nation or sometimes they’re from very far away
places but not only just meeting them as far as meeting
new people, but other contacts and some work that we
can do together.
DONNA: 	And that’s another thing too, with the
beauty of technology. 	It does not matter at all where
someone lives.
LORI: 	Right.
DONNA: 	You know, there is a web designer I
know who’s very good and he’s reliable and he’s based
in England.
DENISE: 	Oh gosh.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 47 of 51 --

48
DONNA: 	And it’s great because you know, he’s
very accessible through email. 	He gets back to me
immediately and with that five hour time difference we
have, if my client gets back to me with changes that
she wants to her site, I just email Richard. By 10
o’clock, 11 o’clock the next day the changes are done.
DENISE: 	That’s perfect.
DONNA: 	So, my client’s thrilled, because it’s
like, “Oh my God, your guy is so responsive” and
everybody’s happy and it works out beautifully.
LORI: 	One thing we didn’t tell everybody is
that we’ve never met each other ---
DONNA: 	That’s right.
LORI: 	--- in real life yet.
DONNA: 	No we haven’t.
LORI: 	I’ve tried a couple of times but you
know, and I’m sure you have people like this in your
life Donna, and I meant it when I said it, not only are
you making wonderful business connections and
partnerships, but also people that share the same
passion in your life, that’s really fun and exciting
when you’re working at home or in your own studio on
your own, is to have these other people in your life
that interject, you know, “Did you see this article?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 48 of 51 --

49
Did you know this new software?” 	That can really help
you out, but you know, finding people that share this
passion but we’ve tried a couple of times to meet each
other, but it just hasn’t worked out, but soon, right?
DONNA: 	That’s right, absolutely.
DENISE: 	You are living proof that you can have
a successful collaboration and do that online and via
the telephone. 	I’d also encourage folks if they’re
interested in beginning to build their network of
partners to start a discussion on the forum, the AWAI
student forum, a member forum. That would be a useful
place to go. 	Lori, can you tell us a little bit about
the graphic design teleconference series?

direct mail today. We’re going to spend some time with
him learning about his secrets to success and then the
final one is with Sandy Franks from Agora who kind of
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

gives you an inside look at what goes on in these big
